items.q: What does negative feedback do in homeostasis?
items.correct: It reverses a change in a controlled variable, bringing it back towards the set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback makes a process get worse or amplifies the change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback stops the body from responding to any internal change.
items.why: The word 'negative' has bad connotations, so students think it makes things worse, missing that it counteracts deviations.

items.q: Why is a reflex action faster than a conscious response?
items.correct: The signal does not need to be processed by the brain — it travels through a simpler reflex arc via the spinal cord.
items.misconception: The brain decides reflexes faster than other actions because it skips thinking.
items.random_wrong: Reflexes are faster because they use specially fast neurones not used elsewhere.
items.why: Students assume the brain is always in charge; they don't realise reflexes specifically bypass the brain to save time.

items.q: How does sweating help cool you down?
items.correct: Evaporation of sweat absorbs energy (latent heat) from the skin, lowering body temperature.
items.misconception: Sweat is cold and directly cools the skin on contact.
items.random_wrong: Sweating removes excess heat by carrying it out as liquid waste.
items.why: Students don't connect sweating to evaporation; they think the liquid itself is the cooling agent, not the phase change.

items.q: Does sweat cool you down simply because it is cold liquid on the skin?
items.correct: No — cooling happens as sweat evaporates, taking heat energy from the skin.
items.misconception: Sweat cools you because the liquid itself is cold.
items.random_wrong: Sweating removes heat by carrying it away as liquid waste.
items.why: Students think the sweat's temperature does the cooling, but it is the evaporation (which needs energy from the skin) that lowers body temperature.

items.q: Does negative feedback make a change worse?
items.correct: No — negative feedback opposes and reverses a change, returning the condition to its set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback amplifies the original change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback switches off all responses in the body.
items.why: The term 'negative' misleads students; it means the response counteracts the change, which is the basis of homeostatic control.

items.q: Which is NOT something the body regulates by homeostasis?
items.why: It's Eye colour. “Body temperature” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a reflex arc, what sits between a sensory neurone and a motor neurone?
items.why: It's Relay neurone. “Brain” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A reflex arc uses 3 neurones (sensory, relay, motor). How many synapses does the signal cross?
items.why: The real figure is 2. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
